questions.question: What is the key principle of A/B testing during the sprint?

options.text: Test as many variables as possible simultaneously
Change one variable at a time so you can attribute improvements
Only test after the sprint is complete

questions.explanation: Change one variable at a time. If you change subject line AND first line AND CTA simultaneously, you won't know what caused any improvement or decline. Controlled experiments require isolating variables.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of contacts per variant needed for reliable A/B test results?
questions.explanation: You need at minimum 30 contacts per variant. With 50 per variant you get a more reliable signal. With fewer than 30, any difference could be random noise.

questions.question: At the mid-sprint point, when should you pivot your strategy?

options.text: Whenever lagging metrics are below target
Only if leading indicators (reply rate) drop below target for 2+ consecutive weeks
Every week to test new approaches
Never — always finish the original plan

questions.explanation: Stay the course if leading indicators (reply rate) are on target. Meetings and closes have a 2-3 week lag from outreach. Only adjust if reply rate drops below target for 2+ consecutive weeks. Most founders pivot too early based on lagging indicators.

questions.question: If your Week 2 reply rate improved from 3.4% to 4.1%, you should immediately scale volume.
questions.explanation: False. Two weeks of improving data is encouraging but Week 2 is about running controlled experiments, not scaling. Scale in Week 3 once you've confirmed which variants work and have enough data to make reliable conclusions.

questions.question: Design an A/B test for your outreach. What single variable will you test, what are your two variants, and how many contacts per variant will you need? How will you determine the winner?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response identifies one specific variable to test (subject line, first line, CTA), describes two distinct variants, specifies a minimum sample size of 30+ per variant, and defines a clear success metric (reply rate, positive reply rate) for determining the winner.
